title: SEGMENT 10 — Agricultural antibiotic use: the unspoken half of the problem

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Agricultural antibiotic use: the unspoken half of the problem
Welcome back.
We're going to spend this segment on something that surprises most students when they first encounter it: roughly half of all antibiotics used in the United States are not given to humans at all.
They are given to food animals — cattle, pigs, chickens, turkeys — most of them at low doses, mixed into feed, for purposes that have nothing to do with treating sick animals.

This is the agricultural half of the antibiotic resistance problem, and it has been, until very recently, almost entirely outside the conversation that physicians and the lay public have about resistance.
The numbers, briefly.

The FDA's most recent reports on antimicrobial sales for use in food-producing animals estimate that around 11 to 13 million kilograms of antibiotic active ingredient are sold annually in the US for animal use, compared with roughly 3 to 4 million kilograms for human use.
The exact ratio fluctuates and depends on how you count, but the basic picture — agricultural antibiotic use is the larger share of total antibiotic consumption — has been stable for decades.
Globally, the imbalance is similar or worse: the OECD estimates that more than two-thirds of total global antimicrobial consumption is in animal agriculture.
There are three main reasons antibiotics are given to food animals, and you should keep them distinct, because they have different policy implications.
The first is therapeutic — actually treating sick animals.
This is uncontroversial and would be defended by essentially everyone.
The use is similar to human use, at full doses, for defined courses of treatment.

The second is prophylactic or metaphylactic — giving antibiotics to groups of animals at risk of infection (after a stressful transport event, for example, or during weaning) to prevent disease from breaking out.
This is more controversial because the doses are often subtherapeutic and the duration extended, but the practice is still defended on welfare grounds in some cases.
The third — historically the largest by volume in the US — is growth promotion.

For reasons that were never fully understood mechanistically (though it almost certainly involves the gut microbiome and the animal's energy balance), feeding subtherapeutic doses of antibiotics to livestock causes them to gain weight faster on the same feed.
This was first observed in the late 1940s with chickens fed antibiotic-fermentation byproducts, and from the 1950s onward it became standard practice in industrial animal agriculture.
The animals get a constant low-dose stream of antibiotics — tetracyclines, sulfonamides, penicillins, sometimes ionophores — for most of their lives, and they grow faster, and producers save money.
The problem, of course, is that constant low-dose antibiotic exposure of bacterial populations is the exact selective pressure that drives resistance evolution.
It's not even a debate at this point.

Decades of research have established beyond serious doubt that agricultural antibiotic use selects for resistant bacteria in animal guts, that those resistant bacteria reach humans through meat consumption, through environmental runoff, and through direct contact with farm workers, and that resistance genes selected in agricultural settings end up in human clinical isolates.
The colistin resistance gene mcr-1 (next segment) was selected in pigs in China before it ever became a human clinical problem.
The original tetracycline resistance plasmids spread first through agricultural use.
The chronology is consistent: agricultural use precedes and seeds human clinical resistance, for class after class.
The policy response.
The European Union banned the use of antibiotics for growth promotion in 2006 (Denmark had banned it nationally in 1995-2000, with measured reductions in resistance prevalence following).
The US FDA finalized rules in 2017 that ended growth-promotion uses of medically important antibiotics — that is, antibiotics also used in humans — though prophylactic uses remain.
These are partial wins.
Total antibiotic use in US animal agriculture has come down meaningfully since 2015.

But globally, especially in China, India, and Brazil — the countries with the largest growing meat production sectors — agricultural antibiotic use is still rising, and is projected to keep rising through 2030.
The framing I want you to take from this segment: agricultural antibiotic use is the unspoken half of the problem, and it cannot be ignored in any serious resistance-control strategy.
The bacteria in a pig's gut do not respect species barriers.
The plasmids that evolve there can and do end up in human pathogens.
When we talk about stewardship in Segment 16, agricultural stewardship is going to be at least half of what we mean.
